claims.text: Over 50% of tech professionals combine multiple AI assistants for specific use cases, such as ChatGPT plus Claude for thought partnership and ChatGPT plus Perplexity for deep research.
claims.evidence: Survey of 6,500 tech professionals showing people mix and match AI tools based on each assistant's strengths.

claims.text: Figma is used by 97% of designers as their primary design tool, while Canva is democratizing design for non-designers like PMs, marketers, and engineers.
claims.evidence: Survey data from 6,500 respondents; 90% of overall participants use Figma.

newsletters.title: Introducing Core 4: The best way to measure and improve your product velocity
newsletters.subtitle: A unified developer productivity framework from the creators of DORA, SPACE, and DevEx

claims.text: Core 4 is a unified developer productivity framework with four dimensions — Speed, Effectiveness, Quality, and Impact — that unifies the principles behind DORA, SPACE, and DevEx.
claims.evidence: Co-authored by Abi Noda and Laura Tacho in collaboration with Nicole Forsgren (DORA founder), Margaret-Anne Storey (SPACE co-author), and Michaela Greiler (DevEx co-author), informed by field experience at over 300 companies.

claims.text: Product velocity is about speed and direction; when teams focus only on moving fast without alignment on what to work on, trying to move faster can actually slow you down.
claims.evidence: Common disagreement between engineering leaders (focused on deployment frequency) and product leaders (focused on deadlines and customer value); Core 4 designed to resolve this tension.

claims.text: Improving the developer experience score (DXI) by one point saves 13 minutes per week per developer, equivalent to 10 hours annually per developer.
claims.evidence: Ongoing research from DX looking at data from 40,000 developers across 800 organizations correlating developer experience with efficiency.

claims.text: The three most common friction points slowing developer teams down are poor build and test processes, lack of time for deep work, and poor support for production debugging.
claims.evidence: Data from hundreds of companies already using Core 4 to identify top friction points in the developer experience.

claims.text: PR throughput per engineer is a controversial but useful metric when used as a system health metric alongside other metrics, as validated by companies like Meta, Microsoft, and Uber.
claims.evidence: Companies like Meta, Microsoft, and Uber have successfully used PRs per engineer as a central part of their productivity measurement; Will Larson (CTO of Carta) endorses comparing this metric across peer companies.

claims.text: Core 4 balances qualitative and quantitative measurements because quantitative data shows what is happening while qualitative insights reveal why, which is necessary to actually change team behavior.
claims.evidence: Design principle of Core 4 framework, supported by Drew Houston (Dropbox CEO) stating it gives a 'much more cohesive picture of what's happening in your organization.'

newsletters.title: Which companies accelerate PM careers most
newsletters.subtitle: A first-ever deep dive into the impact 50+ companies have had on alumni trajectories

claims.text: Revolut, N26, eBay, Plaid, and Intercom are the top five companies that most accelerate PM careers based on promotions, rise to leadership, founder rate, and first-PM rate.
claims.evidence: Analysis of hiring, firing, and promotion data for 160 million people from Live Data Technologies, examining six career trajectory metrics across 50+ companies.

claims.text: Fintech companies dominate the top 10 PM career accelerators with 5 out of 10 spots, likely because fintech work is on hard mode with complex risk, regulatory, and compliance challenges.
claims.evidence: Data analysis showed Revolut, N26, Plaid, Intuit, and Deel in top 10; Dennis Yang suggests fintech nurtures strong leaders due to difficult stakeholders and complicated tradeoffs.

claims.text: FAANG companies lag behind companies like eBay, Intuit, and LinkedIn in accelerating PM careers, possibly because PMs learn to operate well within that specific company but are less successful elsewhere.
claims.evidence: Data shows eBay, Intuit, and LinkedIn far ahead of Microsoft, Google, Apple, Meta, and Amazon across promotion rate, leadership trajectory, and founder rate metrics.

claims.text: Palantir is the standout company for fostering founders, with almost a quarter of its PM alumni going on to start their own company.
claims.evidence: Palantir ranked first by far in founder rate among all companies analyzed, based on Live Data Technologies career trajectory data.

newsletters.title: How GiveDirectly increased donations by over $3 million/year through experimentation
newsletters.subtitle: Wins, misses, and lessons from GiveDirectly’s donation-optimizing journey

claims.text: Adding one-click PayPal and mobile wallet checkout options increased GiveDirectly's online donations by 14%, raising an incremental $1.3 million per year.
claims.evidence: GiveDirectly's A/B testing showed that reducing steps to complete a donation via Apple Pay, Google Pay, and PayPal drove the biggest single lift; 20% of online donations now come through mobile wallets.

claims.text: Changing email opt-in checkbox copy to be transparent about email frequency and value doubled the subscription rate from 25% to 55%, driving an estimated $400,000 per year in incremental donations.
claims.evidence: GiveDirectly tested new checkbox copy that was upfront about how many emails donors would receive and why they should subscribe; subscribers retain 2-3x longer than non-subscribers.

claims.text: A polished homepage redesign with an embedded donation form made visitors 35% more likely to donate, with 20% of the lift from the embedded form and 15% from the design improvement.
claims.evidence: GiveDirectly ran a three-arm A/B test comparing old homepage, new design with button, and new design with embedded form; conversion increased from 1.98% to 2.67%.

claims.text: Setting the default donation frequency to monthly instead of one-time was more effective than using an interruptive modal, because smart defaults work better than adding friction.
claims.evidence: A modal asking to switch to monthly made people 7x more likely to go monthly but decreased one-time donations by 10%, netting negative; simply changing the default to monthly had a positive net effect.

claims.text: Donor-recipient individual matching did not improve conversion or long-term retention despite being a common nonprofit strategy, but matching donors to a specific village had a 58% higher click-through rate at far lower operational cost.
claims.evidence: Two-month A/B homepage test showed no significant conversion difference (p=0.69) for individual matching; retention gains faded over time; village-level matching email had 58% higher CTR and 2x positive written responses.

claims.text: Teams should balance small optimizations with big bets, because low-hanging fruit like checkout improvements can have a bigger payoff than expected while maintaining team morale when bigger experiments fail.
claims.evidence: GiveDirectly's small optimizations (payment options, copy changes, defaults) collectively drove $3M+/year while the big donor-matching bet was ultimately deprecated.

newsletters.title: How to become a supermanager with AI
newsletters.subtitle: AI is ushering in an era of “super ICs.” What about managers?

claims.text: Managers can scale their coaching by distilling their most common feedback into custom GPTs, allowing team members to receive feedback multiple times a day rather than once or twice a week.
claims.evidence: Hilary Gridley at WHOOP created 'The Executive Editor' GPT encoding her writing feedback; team members keep the tab open at all times and report reduced need to run things by their manager.

claims.text: If you are skilled at explaining something to people, you are well equipped to teach it to an AI, making great managers uniquely suited to creating effective custom GPTs.
claims.evidence: Hilary Gridley's experience showing that the skills that make great managers — providing clear feedback and articulating the difference between good and excellent — translate directly to creating GPTs.

claims.text: If 2024 was the year of the super IC, 2025 will be the year of the supermanager who harnesses AI to oversee larger, flatter teams and extend coaching beyond just direct reports.
claims.evidence: WHOOP product team achieved all-time highs in performance, productivity, and NPS after fully embracing AI as a management tool; team described 10x improvements in efficiency and skill.

claims.text: Teams should treat AI as a true thought partner through back-and-forth iteration rather than relying on copy-pasted single-use prompts or pre-built GPTs.
claims.evidence: Hilary Gridley created a 30-day 'Collaborate with AI' program with micro-assignments; team members report using AI to weigh trade-offs and anticipate questions, bringing more refined ideas to discussions.

claims.text: You can reverse-engineer your own managerial intuition into a custom GPT by uploading examples of high-quality and low-quality work, asking the AI to identify specific differences, and then creating a prompt that transforms bad examples into good ones.
claims.evidence: Step-by-step process used by Hilary Gridley to create GPTs that replicate her feedback; team gets AI to handle 0-to-80 feedback so the manager can focus on 80-to-100.

newsletters.title: On being funny at work
newsletters.subtitle: Seven strategies (and some stories) to help you harness the power of humor

claims.text: Humor is a learned skill, not a natural talent, and can be developed through studying and practicing specific strategies.
claims.evidence: Author identifies four types of people who master humor, all involving deliberate practice or obsessive study rather than innate ability.

claims.text: Forbes identified a sense of humor as the fourth most important quality in a leader.
claims.evidence: Cited Forbes ranking of leadership qualities.

claims.text: Laughter alters brain chemistry by swapping cortisol with dopamine, oxytocin, and endorphins, which enhance learning, connection, and pleasure.
claims.evidence: Neurochemical explanation of how laughter produces dopamine (learning/attention), oxytocin (empathy/connection), and endorphins (pleasure).

claims.text: The core mechanism of humor is surprise, achieved through the strategic assembly of specific words spoken in a specific way.
claims.evidence: Analysis of comedy examples from Wanda Sykes, Christopher Walken, and others showing how unexpected elements produce laughs.

claims.text: Specificity in language increases humor potential and is risk-free because failed specific details are perceived as informative rather than as failed jokes.
claims.evidence: Examples of how 'Jack Russell terrier' is funnier than 'dog' and brand names like 'Vienna Fingers' add humor without downside risk.

newsletters.title: Five proven prompt engineering techniques (and a few more-advanced tactics)
newsletters.subtitle: How to get exactly what you want when working with AI

claims.text: The difference between mediocre and mind-blowing AI results comes down to prompt crafting, not the model itself.
claims.evidence: Author contrasts his own meh ChatGPT results with people building entire products and saving hundreds of hours, attributing the gap to prompt quality.

claims.text: Role-playing, style unbundling, emotion prompting, few-shot learning, and synthetic bootstrap are the five most consistently useful prompt engineering techniques.
claims.evidence: Professional prompt engineer Mike Taylor selected these five from hundreds of techniques based on daily use and scientific backing from ArXiv papers.

claims.text: Adding emotional stakes to a prompt (e.g. 'This task is very important for my career') can elicit more careful and thoughtful AI responses.
claims.evidence: Emotion prompting technique backed by research (arxiv.org/abs/2307.11760) showing emotional context improves output quality.

claims.text: Splitting AI tasks into multiple steps with evaluation loops outperforms trying to get everything right in a single prompt.
claims.evidence: Advanced tactics like chain-of-thought, RAG, and LLM-as-a-judge all involve breaking work into steps to correct common AI errors.

claims.text: While AI won't replace you directly, those who use AI effectively will have an advantage in promotions and job applications.
claims.evidence: Author's conclusion that mastering prompt engineering provides competitive career advantage as AI continues to evolve.

newsletters.title: How much do U.S. product managers really make?
newsletters.subtitle: The rise of ICs, the unexpected salary dip for managers, why salary only moderately impacts satisfaction, and more revealing insights from our recent compensation survey.

claims.text: Senior individual contributor PMs often out-earn managers, with senior ICs earning a median of $187,500 vs. $162,500 for managers.
claims.evidence: Survey of 5,000+ respondents showing senior ICs earn $26,920 more in median salary than managers, challenging the assumption that management always pays more.

claims.text: The correlation between total compensation and job satisfaction is only moderate (0.31), meaning more money does not strongly predict happiness in a role.
claims.evidence: Survey data showing a 0.31 correlation coefficient between total comp package and satisfaction, and negotiating comp showed no significant impact on satisfaction.

claims.text: Job level is one of the strongest predictors of PM salary, far more than years of experience, which is actually an anti-pattern for predicting compensation.
claims.evidence: Pave CEO Matt Schulman explains that a PM with 10 years of experience could be IC5 at one company and IC3 at another, making level the real driver.

claims.text: The rise of the IC career path is reinforced by recent layoffs disproportionately affecting middle managers, flattening organizations.
claims.evidence: Trend data from 5,000+ survey responses plus Mark Zuckerberg's 2023 push for employees to 'get back to making things.'

claims.text: Entry-level IC PMs earn around $112,500, mid-level ICs earn $137,500, and senior ICs earn $187,500, showing nearly $78K growth across the IC track.
claims.evidence: Median salary data from the Lenny's Newsletter compensation survey of U.S. product managers (n=1,740).

newsletters.title: Why no productivity hack will solve your overwhelm
newsletters.subtitle: How the Internal Family Systems model can help you listen to your inner wisdom and get unstuck

claims.text: Inner conflict between competing internal parts is the major hidden driver of productivity and burnout issues at work, not a lack of productivity tactics.
claims.evidence: Executive coach Natalie Rothfels observes that clients can recite every productivity tactic but remain unproductive due to people-pleasing and poor internal awareness.

claims.text: Internal Family Systems (IFS) is an effective method for resolving workplace stuckness by identifying and facilitating dialogue between conflicting internal parts.
claims.evidence: Case study of Gustavo, a Director of Product who shifted from seeking time management tools to building tolerance of chaos by recognizing his competing internal parts.

claims.text: The parts of ourselves that we judge, shame, or ignore tend to get louder and more extreme, while parts that feel heard become flexible and trusting.
claims.evidence: IFS principle demonstrated through coaching examples where validating internal experiences creates shifts without changing external circumstances.

claims.text: Building internal capacity for navigating inner conflict also develops significantly more empathy and tolerance for external team conflict, a critical leadership skill.
claims.evidence: Author connects internal parts-work to improved radar for team polarization and better facilitation of productive dialogue in teams.

newsletters.title: Why cash is king
newsletters.subtitle: The first set of results from our inaugural compensation survey reveals the need for stable income above all else

claims.text: 75% of tech professionals now prioritize salary over equity when evaluating job offers, a significant shift from the traditional 'maximize equity' wisdom.
claims.evidence: Survey of 5,000+ Lenny's Newsletter subscribers; only 13% selected equity over salary.

claims.text: Equity skepticism is widespread, with many tech workers describing equity as 'Monopoly money' or 'a lottery ticket' after past experiences where equity failed to deliver.
claims.evidence: Qualitative survey responses from PMs, engineers, and VPs across company stages consistently expressing distrust of equity value.

claims.text: Men are 2.3 times as likely as women to prefer equity over salary, and each increase in seniority level increases equity preference by a factor of 2.0.
claims.evidence: Logistic regression analysis of survey data showing gender and job level as strongest predictors of equity preference.

claims.text: U.S.-based employees are 1.75 times as likely to prioritize equity as those outside the U.S., while EU respondents are 42% less likely to prefer equity.
claims.evidence: Geographic analysis from logistic regression showing statistically significant location-based differences in compensation preferences.

claims.text: Prioritizing salary over equity is not necessarily short-sighted because a strong base salary amplifies overall compensation through percentage-based bonuses and raises.
claims.evidence: Engineering manager example showing bonuses structured as percentages of base salary, plus salary setting the bar for future roles.

newsletters.title: A PM’s guide to influence
newsletters.subtitle: How to align stakeholders so decisions go your way

claims.text: The first step to influencing stakeholders is gathering intel on how each person makes decisions, including their goals, incentives, fears, and who they consult.
claims.evidence: Jules Walter's approach at YouTube of conducting 1-on-1s with key stakeholders to learn their OKRs, decision processes, and key decision makers before making any ask.

claims.text: Framing proposals from the stakeholder's perspective rather than your own is dramatically more effective because people are more receptive to proposals that address what is top of mind for them.
claims.evidence: At Slack, framing a monetization experiment around user benefits (CEO's priority) rather than revenue impact got CEO approval and led to 20% increase in paying teams.

claims.text: You should prime both detractors and champions before key decision meetings rather than hoping the meeting goes well, building a coalition of supporters in advance.
claims.evidence: Jules held 14+ pre-meetings before a YouTube strategy summit, converting some detractors to supporters, and the plan was voted top 3 across YouTube.

claims.text: People who feel dismissed or misunderstood enter a stress response state and become more defensive, making them resistant to influence and unlikely to reciprocate.
claims.evidence: Psychological principle applied to stakeholder management: playing back statements in your own words makes people feel heard and more open to influence.

claims.text: Managing the clock in decision meetings is critical because with senior executives, if you don't exit with a decision, you may not get another meeting for weeks or months.
claims.evidence: Jules states explicit meeting goals up front to gain legitimacy for steering conversation, and adapts format (memos, silent reading, skipping context slides) by audience.

newsletters.title: State of the product job market, part 2
newsletters.subtitle: Which companies are hiring PMs, what hard skills are most in demand, trends in remote work, whether there’s been a shift to senior hires, what roles AI companies are hiring for, and so much more

claims.text: Remote PM jobs have declined approximately 35% from their peak, dropping from over 35% of open roles to 22.5%.
claims.evidence: TrueUp data tracking open PM roles globally showing steady decline in remote-friendly positions over two years.

claims.text: Over one in five open PM roles is in the San Francisco Bay Area, with the share growing 25% year over year from 15.4% to over 20%.
claims.evidence: TrueUp job opening data showing SF Bay Area's increasing dominance in PM hiring despite remote work trends.

claims.text: The three most common hard skills hiring managers look for from PMs are SQL, Jira, and experience with LLMs, with LLMs being the fastest-growing requirement.
claims.evidence: Analysis of skill mentions in PM job descriptions from TrueUp data, with LLMs growing fastest while SQL remains most mentioned overall.

claims.text: Machine-learning engineers and data engineers are the fastest-growing tech roles, growing 79% and 55% year over year respectively.
claims.evidence: TrueUp data on tech company hiring trends showing AI-related engineering roles accelerating on top of prior year growth.

claims.text: Diversity and scrum master roles are the fastest-shrinking in tech, with only 70 open diversity roles and 74 scrum master roles (none at FAANG).
claims.evidence: TrueUp data on role categories at tech companies showing these two categories declining fastest.

claims.text: AI companies are hiring almost exclusively engineers and data scientists; there are only 456 open AI PM roles out of 16,935 total AI company openings.
claims.evidence: TrueUp data on AI company job openings showing engineering-heavy hiring with very limited PM positions.

newsletters.title: Raising a seed round 101
newsletters.subtitle: The whys, whats, and hows of seed funding, with advice from the founders of Notion, Linear, Figma, Ramp, Instacart, 37signals, and more

claims.text: You should aim for 24 to 36 months of runway with a 25% buffer when raising a seed round, as the median time from seed to Series A is 23 months.
claims.evidence: Formula based on typical time to product-market fit; Carta data shows median seed-to-Series-A timeline of 23 months.

claims.text: Most seed rounds are now $2M to $4M with a typical post-money valuation of around $20M and approximately 15% equity sold.
claims.evidence: Market data cited by investors who have collectively seen over 1,000 seed fundraises.

claims.text: Creating FOMO among investors by compressing your fundraise into a 2-3 week window and collecting smaller angel checks first is the most effective strategy for raising a seed round.
claims.evidence: Advice from investors with 1,000+ seed round experience: time compression creates desire and scarcity, while early angel checks build social proof for larger investors.

claims.text: Raising money too early teaches founders how to spend rather than earn, and the skill of making more than you spend takes considerable practice time to master.
claims.evidence: Jason Fried of 37signals/Basecamp advises that bootstrapping forces practicing revenue generation, a skill that won't develop when flush with investor cash.

claims.text: Who introduces you to prospective investors matters far more than most founders realize, with successful portfolio founders being the highest-influence intro source.
claims.evidence: High-influence intros include portfolio founders, other successful founders, and existing investors; investors who are NOT investing are negative signals.

claims.text: A founder's own conviction across commitment, work, and insight is perhaps the single most predictive variable in raising seed capital.
claims.evidence: Authors state that if you truly believe in the opportunity, you will likely be able to convince someone to give you money, based on 1,000+ seed rounds observed.

newsletters.title: How to communicate tradeoffs so leaders will listen
newsletters.subtitle: Tactical tips for teeing up critical decisions without pissing leaders off

claims.text: Maintaining a public ongoing stack rank (OSR) of all projects changes the conversation from 'Why can't we do this one extra thing?' to 'Do you agree that X is more important than Y?'
claims.evidence: Tara Seshan's OSR framework at Stripe, presented at every opportunity, made priorities common knowledge and reframed tradeoff discussions.

claims.text: Steelmanning incoming requests by presenting the strongest possible version of the opposing argument before countering it makes you more persuasive and minimizes surprise in executive meetings.
claims.evidence: Amazon PM example where steelmanning Bezos's '?' email about screws revealed a much larger class of specification-driven search problems worth solving.

claims.text: Framing tradeoffs in terms of company goals rather than team goals is essential because leadership always thinks company-first and is more likely to side with arguments presented that way.
claims.evidence: Author's Stripe Billing experience where reframing team goals in context of global expansion (company goal) finally got requests prioritized.

claims.text: Adding more engineers is never a good solution to a prioritization problem because planning will always scale to match capacity, and you will still have more requests than you can satisfy.
claims.evidence: The 'just one more engineer' trap illustrated by the Google Video vs. YouTube acquisition story where the product lead thought one more engineer could beat YouTube.

claims.text: PMs should always communicate an opinionated recommendation rather than presenting two unbiased options, because they have more context than leadership on execution details.
claims.evidence: Author uses SCQA framework with bottom-line-up-front decision documents and structured meetings to present and defend specific recommendations.

claims.text: Predicting long-term consequences of short-term requests is a PM superpower because leadership teams want to do the right long-term thing but may be held back by short-term fears.
claims.evidence: Watershed example where PM Steve proposed automation over manual processes, laying out 3- and 15-month outlooks that made the long-term path the top company priority.

newsletters.title: The Subscription Value Loop: A framework for growing consumer subscription businesses
newsletters.subtitle: Including case studies, templates, and 10 proven growth tactics from top consumer subscription apps like Duolingo, Spotify, Tinder, and Calm

claims.text: Even top-quartile consumer subscription apps only convert roughly 1 in 20 installs into a paid subscription and lose more than half of annual subscribers after the first year.
claims.evidence: Data cited from RevenueCat benchmarks showing median and top-quartile conversion and retention rates across consumer subscription apps.

claims.text: The best consumer subscription apps grow by building on a core value promise that drives a compounding Subscription Value Loop across three steps: Value Creation, Value Delivery, and Value Capture.
claims.evidence: Framework developed by guest author Phil Carter based on experience running hundreds of A/B tests across dozens of consumer subscription businesses.

claims.text: Pricing and packaging optimizations consistently generate 5% to 15% lift on subscription revenue, yet many startups set prices once and never revisit them for years.
claims.evidence: Phil Carter's experience across multiple consumer subscription businesses and the AllTrails case study showing an 8.3% ARPU lift from a price increase informed by Van Westendorp analysis.

claims.text: More than 75% of trial starts occur on the same day a user installs an app, often in the first few minutes, making speed-to-value in onboarding critical for conversion.
claims.evidence: Recent industry reports cited in the article about trial start timing relative to install date.

claims.text: Web conversion flows can boost subscriber LTV by 30% to 50% by avoiding app store fees and reducing involuntary churn from payment failures.
claims.evidence: Ketty Slonimsky, Chief Growth Officer at Palta (Flo, Simple, Zing), reported consistently seeing these LTV improvements with strong execution.

claims.text: Twenty to forty percent of churn in consumer subscription businesses is involuntary churn caused by payment failures, expired cards, and processing errors rather than deliberate cancellation.
claims.evidence: Estimates from Paddle and Recurly cited in the article, supported by Spotify's success reducing involuntary churn through local payment methods.

claims.text: Duolingo grew DAUs 4.5x from 2018 to 2022 by focusing on current user retention rate and investing in motivation tactics like streaks, badges, and leaderboards.
claims.evidence: Former CPO Jorge Mazal's account of Duolingo's growth strategy and former VP Gina Gotthilf's description of gamification features driving DAU growth.

claims.text: Consumer subscription apps should target a Day 1 Paywall View Rate at or above 80% to maximize conversion opportunities.
claims.evidence: Guidance from Superwall CEO Jake Mor on paywall optimization, detailing four common paywall trigger points.

newsletters.title: Part 2: Time management techniques that actually work
newsletters.subtitle: Seven more tactics for making the most of your precious time

claims.text: Setting arbitrary deadlines with collaborators creates a gravitational pull toward completion even when both parties know the deadline is made up.
claims.evidence: Lenny's personal experience using the technique, noting people hit these arbitrary deadlines surprisingly often when they choose the date themselves.

claims.text: Writing to-dos on pen and paper and rewriting them each morning on a fresh page is more effective than digital to-do apps because physical lists cannot be swiped away.
claims.evidence: Lenny's personal practice of tearing out yesterday's page and transferring still-relevant items, which forces daily re-prioritization.

claims.text: You can often back out of commitments your past self agreed to with minimal consequences, and the relief from doing so helps you get better at saying no in the first place.
claims.evidence: Lenny provides three template scripts of varying intensity for backing out of commitments, from lightweight rescheduling to direct cancellation.

claims.text: A 15-minute end-of-day calendar review for the next day transforms morning anxiety from overwhelming to manageable and makes you feel ahead of the curve.
claims.evidence: Lenny's personal practice of reviewing the calendar each afternoon and Sunday evening, inspired by Cal Newport's work shutdown ritual.

claims.text: Conducting both a time audit and an energy audit reveals the gap between your stated priorities and revealed preferences, informing how to restructure your schedule.
claims.evidence: Lenny's recommendation combining a time audit approach with Matt Mochary's energy audit methodology to optimize daily scheduling.

newsletters.title: Why PMs are best positioned to thrive in an AI world
newsletters.subtitle: An optimistic case for the future of product management

claims.text: Product managers are the best-positioned role in tech to thrive in a world of AI because the PM job is an amalgamation of soft skills that AI will have the toughest time replacing.
claims.evidence: Lenny argues PMs excel at communication, collaboration, coordination, influence, taste, and vision, which are harder for AI to replicate than hard skills like coding or data analysis.

claims.text: The most valued skill set in tech will increasingly shift from building to knowing what to build, giving clear instructions, and having the taste to judge quality.
claims.evidence: Lenny's observation that AI tools like Cursor, Devin, and Suno are enabling non-technical people to create music, video, code, and designs by describing what they want.

claims.text: Engineers and designers with the strongest PM-type skills like identifying customer pain points, understanding business levers, and communicating clearly will do best in an AI world.
claims.evidence: Lenny notes product-minded engineers are essentially a PM and engineer in one, and over time they will look more like PMs than engineers.

claims.text: Many jobs across industries will start to look more like product management as AI handles execution and humans focus on directing, iterating, and driving adoption.
claims.evidence: Examples from music (Suno), movies (Pika, Runway, Sora), engineering (Devin, Copilot), and design (Figma AI) where the human role shifts to PM-like direction.

claims.text: A top-tier PM with the skills to fully harness AI capabilities is going to be the most valuable role in tech.
claims.evidence: Lenny's updated PM definition adds marshaling both human and AI resources, supported by Peter Thiel's observation that AI is bad news for math skills and good for soft skills.

newsletters.title: Time management techniques that actually work
newsletters.subtitle: Ten tactics I use every single day

claims.text: Putting to-dos directly on your calendar as events is more effective than using a to-do app because your calendar is your personal roadmap and items not on the roadmap get ignored.
claims.evidence: Technique drawn from Nir Eyal's Indistractable and Jake Knapp's Make Time, validated by Lenny's personal daily use.

claims.text: If a task takes less than two minutes, doing it immediately is more efficient than storing and tracking it in a system.
claims.evidence: David Allen's two-minute rule from Getting Things Done, which Lenny has used for over 15 years since reading the book.

claims.text: Keeping a waiting-for list of every open request you have made to others is one of the most important habits for building an aura of reliability as a PM.
claims.evidence: Adapted from the Getting Things Done framework; Lenny connects it to building the PM aura of 'I got this' that he previously identified as a hallmark of great PMs.

claims.text: Setting up two to three recurring deep work blocks per week and fiercely protecting them from meeting encroachment is essential for getting real work done.
claims.evidence: Inspired by Cal Newport's Deep Work; Lenny describes these blocks as basically the only time he got real work done while working full-time.

claims.text: Most meetings can be replaced with a five-minute async email exchange, saving not just meeting time but also the pre-meeting and post-meeting context-switching overhead.
claims.evidence: Lenny's personal experience that most meeting requests can be redirected with the reply template 'Could we start async over email?'

claims.text: Most burnout and overwhelm comes not from a lack of productivity tools but from taking on too much work, making saying no the most important productivity skill.
claims.evidence: Lenny provides four tactical frameworks for saying no, including filtering through priorities, imagining no emotion involved, and asking 'would I say yes if it were tomorrow?'

newsletters.title: On asking for help (even when you really don’t want to)
newsletters.subtitle: Step-by-step tactics for facing your fears and making the ask—including templates and example scripts

claims.text: Every successful person became successful because they were skilled at asking for help, and failing to build this muscle actively slows your career trajectory.
claims.evidence: Lenny's introduction drawing on conversations with many leaders, reinforced by guest author Natalie Rothfels' coaching experience with product leaders and executives.

claims.text: The fear of appearing incompetent when asking for help is usually unfounded; people rarely perceive someone as weak or burdensome when they make a thoughtful, unentitled ask.
claims.evidence: Natalie Rothfels' client Meena tested this assumption experimentally with PM and engineering peers who empathized rather than judging her.

claims.text: Effective help requests follow a six-part structure: signpost, clear request, rationale, why them, timeline, and opt-in/out.
claims.evidence: Template developed by Natalie Rothfels from her coaching practice, demonstrated through multiple example scripts for peers, managers, and strangers.

claims.text: Product managers' pride in ownership of problems often makes them resist asking for help, but asking for help actually increases agency, builds higher-trust relationships, and models psychological safety.
claims.evidence: Natalie Rothfels' coaching work with product leaders who confuse high ownership with doing everything alone, leading to burnout.

claims.text: There are five distinct types of help you can seek: perspective gathering, information/knowledge, task progression, empathetic support, and advocacy.
claims.evidence: Menu framework built by Natalie Rothfels to help clients clarify what type of help they need, since ambiguity about the ask prevents people from requesting help.

newsletters.title: Ethan Smith: The power of internal linking for SEO
newsletters.subtitle: SEO expert Ethan Smith shares actionable tips for using internal linking to improve your website's search rankings and drive traffic to your business.

claims.text: Internal linking is the highest-ROI quick win for SEO, yet more than 90% of websites are not utilizing it effectively.
claims.evidence: Ethan Smith's estimate from his experience at Graphite working with companies like MasterClass, Medium, and Upwork on SEO optimization.

claims.text: Editorial SEO with manually written content has become more effective at driving traffic than programmatic SEO and is broadly underappreciated.
claims.evidence: Ethan Smith's assessment from his work at Graphite, contrasting human-written articles with automatically generated pages.

claims.text: AI is useful for content workflow steps like extracting structure, identifying topics, and keyword research, but should not be used for the actual writing because factual accuracy cannot be guaranteed.
claims.evidence: Ethan Smith warns that GPT-generated content may produce inaccurate sentences, which harms both Google rankings and user trust, especially in sensitive domains like health.

claims.text: A page's proximity to a high-authority crawl point directly influences its share of link equity, so you want the shortest path from a crawl point to every important page.
claims.evidence: Ethan Smith's four-step internal linking strategy recommending at least 5-10 links per page connecting to best-performing pages.

newsletters.title: How should you monetize your AI features?
newsletters.subtitle: What we can learn from the monetization strategies of leading tech companies, including GitHub, Zapier, Adobe, Loom, and Microsoft

claims.text: 59% of tech companies bundle AI features into existing packages rather than charging separately, making it the predominant monetization strategy.
claims.evidence: Analysis of pricing and bundling strategies of 44 leading tech incumbents at the application layer

claims.text: Direct monetization of AI features is generally the better long-term strategy because indirect monetization makes it very hard to track and accurately attribute value from retention and upsell.
claims.evidence: Author's experience advising 20+ tech companies on monetization, supported by analysis of 44 companies

claims.text: If over 70% of users are likely to use an AI feature, it should be bundled into a standard package; below 70%, consider offering it as an add-on.
claims.evidence: Benchmark from Palle Broe's experience working with companies on packaging decisions

claims.text: AI add-on pricing ranges from 25% of the base package price to 4.75x the cost of the standard SaaS product, with monthly per-user prices spanning $4 to $30.
claims.evidence: Analysis of companies offering AI as add-ons or standalone products including Adobe, GitHub, and Microsoft

claims.text: Per-user monthly fee remains the preferred pricing structure for AI features across nearly all 44 companies reviewed, prioritizing adoption simplicity over usage-based pricing.
claims.evidence: Review of 44 tech companies' AI pricing structures

claims.text: Intercom's pay-per-resolution model for its AI bot Fin is one of the only examples of true pricing model innovation among major tech companies monetizing AI.
claims.evidence: Cited as a standout among 44 companies analyzed for AI pricing approaches

newsletters.title: New data on the product job market
newsletters.subtitle: Who’s hiring, what roles are growing, the one role that’s disappearing, and more surprising insights

claims.text: Growth roles are the fastest-growing product-adjacent roles in the U.S., growing much faster than sales and marketing roles, potentially signaling an increasing focus on product-led growth.
claims.evidence: Data from Live Data Technologies tracking employment changes for 88 million people in the U.S.

claims.text: Product management roles are not shrinking despite layoff fears; there are about 450,000 active PMs in the U.S. with 2,500 to 4,500 being hired each month, holding steady after a 2022 hiring acceleration.
claims.evidence: Live Data Technologies employment tracking data showing hiring and firing rates over 2.5 years

claims.text: User research is the second-fastest-growing role after going through massive layoffs, as companies realized they went too far in cutting UXR teams.
claims.evidence: Live Data Technologies data showing UXR overhiring in 2022, reckoning during market downturn, and now rehiring

claims.text: Scrum master is the only product-adjacent role that is actively shrinking, with more people being let go than hired each month.
claims.evidence: Live Data Technologies employment data; about 40,000 active scrum masters remain in the U.S.

claims.text: Product owner roles are the third-fastest-growing role with around 65,000 active roles, likely partly explained by former scrum masters transitioning to this title.
claims.evidence: Live Data Technologies data showing steady growth with no big spikes or slowdowns

newsletters.title: How close is AI to replacing product managers?
newsletters.subtitle: Closer than you think

claims.text: In blind tests, AI-generated answers beat human PM answers in two out of three core PM tasks (product strategy, defining KPIs, and estimating ROI), with 70-80% of voters correctly identifying the AI answer but still often preferring it.
claims.evidence: Blind polls on X and LinkedIn comparing AI (GPT-4o with expert prompting) vs human answers from Exponent

claims.text: Most people underestimate how close AI is to replacing human work because evaluations typically use outdated models without prompt engineering, which can yield a 50-60% accuracy boost.
claims.evidence: Research paper 'Language Models are Few-Shot Learners' showing prompting drove ~30% improvement, with examples boosting to 50-60%

claims.text: AI's primary weakness in PM tasks is producing tactically comprehensive but not truly strategic answers, while humans win by incorporating unexpected connections, niche references, and genuine strategic thinking.
claims.evidence: Voter comments on blind test where AI product strategy was criticized as a 'list of features rather than a true strategy'

claims.text: Even when AI ties with human performance on PM tasks, it is effectively a win for AI because it costs pennies and takes seconds versus hours of human effort.
claims.evidence: Author's analysis of cost-benefit tradeoffs in the blind evaluation results

newsletters.title: Counterintuitive advice for building AI products
newsletters.subtitle: Lessons from 20+ top product builders, including Scott Belsky, Elad Gil, Rahul Vohra, and Sarah Guo

claims.text: Most early AI apps have a 'tourist' problem with shockingly low retention, and two in five gen AI products still haven't made a single dollar despite millions spent building them.
claims.evidence: Emergence Capital survey finding 60% have integrated gen AI but 2 in 5 products have zero revenue

claims.text: The first-pass AI product is often a bolt-on or simple chat experience; the high-value experience requires a deeper rethink after understanding what the technology really provides.
claims.evidence: Quote from Elad Gil, echoed by multiple AI product builders including leaders at Intercom and Canva

claims.text: Branding AI-powered products as 'AI-powered' increases initial engagement and helps users understand feature capabilities, contrary to the conventional wisdom that users don't care how something is built.
claims.evidence: James Evans (CommandBar CEO) found that chatbots branded as AI-powered get more usage and better usage patterns

claims.text: The smallest and almost invisible AI features like pre-filling names and simple data transformations often have bigger customer adoption than big AI features like chatbots or complex agents.
claims.evidence: Claire Vo (CPO at LaunchDarkly) and Stephen Whitworth (CEO at Incident.io, where 75% of incident summaries are now AI-generated)

claims.text: Experimenting to find the right UI/UX for an AI feature can have an equally big impact on conversion metrics as research updates to the AI model itself.
claims.evidence: Joel Kwartler (head of product at Runway) on how UX improvements boosted conversion even for users already using the capability

claims.text: The best AI features may reduce metrics like time spent in-app because generative AI finds the best product-market fit with products that increase productivity and give time back to people.
claims.evidence: Gaurav Misra (CEO at Captions) contrasting generative AI with previous recommendation/ranking AI systems

claims.text: Companies with proprietary or uniquely structured data sets will have a durable advantage in AI because models are becoming commoditized, and designers will be more important than ever.
claims.evidence: Scott Belsky (Adobe CSO) noting models will run locally on devices within a few years

newsletters.title: General management, functional, and hybrid models: Which org design works best for top companies?
newsletters.subtitle: How stage, North Star metrics, and talent can impact this critical decision

claims.text: Companies almost always start with a functional model and should only consider a GM model after having multiple proven product groups, predictable business outcomes, and significant scale.
claims.evidence: Examples of Block transitioning near IPO, Coinbase in late 2022 with multi-billions in revenue, Airbnb after ~10 years

claims.text: The closer your North Star metric is to revenue, the more suited your organization is to a GM model; companies with metrics removed from direct revenue (like ad-based businesses) work better with functional structures.
claims.evidence: Analysis of Block, Coinbase, Amazon (GM-suited) vs Meta, Google Search (functional-suited) organizational structures

claims.text: Functional models optimize for product growth through cohesive customer journeys that create organic flywheels, as demonstrated by Shopify becoming the sole system of record for many customers after switching from GM to functional.
claims.evidence: Shopify's 2020 shift from GM to functional model enabled seamless merchant experience and organic growth flywheels

claims.text: Most companies in practice operate in a hybrid model that skews toward GM or functional, because achieving a perfect balance is nearly impossible.
claims.evidence: Analysis across Square, Shopify, Dropbox, Meta, Google, Mercury, DoorDash, Canva, Intuit, and Coinbase

newsletters.title: When and how to run a billboard campaign
newsletters.subtitle: Is $20K for a spot on Highway 101 worth it?

claims.text: Out-of-home billboard campaigns can deliver a 25%+ uplift in branded search traffic during the campaign and sustain a 10-15% uplift after billboards come down.
claims.evidence: Stytch's April 2024 San Francisco OOH campaign with 80 bus ads, 130 shelter ads, and 8 billboards

claims.text: Effective out-of-home advertising requires either a lot of placements, an extremely resonant message that goes viral, or a mix of both to achieve recall through frequency and resonance.
claims.evidence: Framework from Kira Klaas (Notion) combined with examples from Vanta (Reddit virality) and Bland (25M video views)

claims.text: Billboard campaigns should not exceed 20% of your marketing budget and work best as brand awareness amplifiers rather than direct demand generation.
claims.evidence: Andrew Racine from Writer suggested 20% maximum; supported by insights from Ramp, Notion, Stytch, and others

claims.text: A Highway 101 billboard in San Francisco costs about $20,000 minimum per month, with high-profile locations running $50K-$80K, delivering 400,000 to 1 million impressions per week.
claims.evidence: Pricing data shared by Stytch based on their campaign experience

newsletters.title: How to use Perplexity in your PM work
newsletters.subtitle: 27 examples (with actual prompts) of how product managers are using the AI-powered search engine today

claims.text: Over 50% of product managers now use an AI chatbot daily, and 85% use one weekly, representing an unprecedented pace of tool adoption in the PM profession.
claims.evidence: Polls on X and LinkedIn conducted by Lenny Rachitsky

claims.text: PMs are primarily using AI search tools like Perplexity for six categories of work: growth strategy, finding benchmarks, market research, learning best practices, evaluating tools, and understanding technical jargon.
claims.evidence: Survey of 300+ PMs with 30 follow-up calls conducted in collaboration with Perplexity

newsletters.title: In defense of feature team product managers
newsletters.subtitle: Why feature team PMs are real PMs

claims.text: Despite empowered teams being dramatically more popular in stated preference, the majority of PMs still operate on feature teams, often for practical reasons like compensation, visa sponsorship, or not knowing what they are missing.
claims.evidence: Ben Erez's analysis from conversations with hundreds of PMs, identifying seven specific reasons PMs stay on feature teams

claims.text: Feature team PMs who try to behave like empowered PMs without executive support typically deplete their social capital, get perceived as slowing velocity, and get dinged on performance reviews.
claims.evidence: Pattern observed firsthand by Ben Erez and reported by many other PMs, described in a five-step failure sequence

claims.text: Feature factories can be the right choice for some CEOs, especially visionary founders with a clear view of what needs to be built, and VCs often back these types of leaders pattern-matching to Elon Musk and Steve Jobs.
claims.evidence: Ben Erez's experience as founder, first PM at three startups, and senior PM at Facebook and Attentive

claims.text: The product work on feature teams and empowered teams is largely the same (PRDs, design partnership, research, sales enablement), with empowered teams adding opportunity identification and post-launch iteration.
claims.evidence: Detailed comparison of PM activities on both team types from Ben Erez's direct experience on both

newsletters.title: On saying no
newsletters.subtitle: Strategies, templates, and advice on how to stay focused while still being kind

claims.text: The key test for whether to say yes to an opportunity is asking 'Would I be excited about this if it were tomorrow?' because it is incredibly easy to say yes to things months in the future that you will regret.
claims.evidence: Lenny's personal practice for managing 100+ daily emails, dozens of DMs, and 20+ Slack messages

claims.text: Creating and communicating personal policies like 'I don't do talks or events' is more effective than evaluating each request individually, because people understand and accept policies without taking offense.
claims.evidence: Lenny's experience managing requests as a solopreneur, with templates and TextExpander shortcuts

claims.text: Not replying at all often feels better to the recipient than a half-hearted reply, because busy people know you are busy and will assume you did not see the message or it was not a fit.
claims.evidence: Lenny's personal experience managing overwhelming inbound communication volume

claims.text: People who become successful quickly often disappear just as fast because they cannot resist the distraction of new opportunities that take time away from the work that made them successful.
claims.evidence: Paraphrased insight attributed to Naval Ravikant, supported by Warren Buffett quote on saying no

podcasts.title: Jenny Wen
podcasts.description: It's not just designers who are feeling like, "Oh yeah, we have to keep up with engineers." I think even engineers are like, "How do we keep up with ourselves?", covering product design, AI product work, and engineering tradeoffs.

moments.speaker: Jenny Wen
moments.text: This design process that designers have been taught, where you go off and do a bunch of research and discovery, and then you diverge, converge, diverge, converge — we sort of treated as gospel. That's basically dead.
moments.summary: The traditional design process of diverge-converge cycles that designers treated as gospel is now dead in the age of AI.

moments.speaker: Jenny Wen
moments.text: There are basically two types of design work now. The first is supporting implementation and execution — engineers are using their seven Claudes to create features. The second is creating the vision or direction, but now it's a three to six month vision, not a two to five year one, and it's sometimes just creating a prototype that points people in the right direction.
moments.summary: Design work has stratified into two modes: supporting fast engineering execution and setting short-term vision through prototypes rather than beautiful long-term decks.

moments.speaker: Jenny Wen
moments.text: It's not just designers who are feeling like, 'Oh yeah, we have to keep up with engineers.' I think even engineers are like, 'How do we keep up with ourselves?'
moments.summary: The pace of AI-driven engineering change is so fast that even engineers are struggling to keep up with their own capabilities.

moments.speaker: Jenny Wen
moments.text: The way you really lose trust around quality and releasing something early is if you release it early and then nothing ever happens. But whenever you put something out early, it's possible to maintain the brand of your company if you commit to iterating, responding to feedback, and continuously shipping improvements.
moments.summary: Shipping early is fine for brand trust as long as you visibly iterate and respond to user feedback — what kills trust is releasing something early and then going silent.

moments.speaker: Jenny Wen
moments.text: I think AI will get better at taste and judgment and design. We might be holding onto that a little bit too much. At the end of the day, someone has to decide what is actually going to get built and what actually matters. Someone still needs to be accountable for the decision.
moments.summary: AI will improve at taste and design judgment, but humans will still be needed for accountability and deciding what actually matters to build.

moments.speaker: Jenny Wen
moments.text: Being an IC across this past year gave me so many hard skills I wouldn't have gained if I was just managing. The design process has changed so much that I think design managers need to move back into IC work to truly understand what is happening, so they can be better managers.
moments.summary: Design managers should rotate back into IC work to build empathy and hard skills for the rapidly changing AI-era design process.

podcasts.title: Jeetu Patel
podcasts.description: And the reason we pulled this thing together, the goal was, what is happening in the industry and how can we help customers make sure that they can make the most of it, covering AI product work, team leadership, and product design.

moments.speaker: Jeetu Patel
moments.text: You have to know the difference between a megatrend and a hype cycle. When there's a megatrend, don't fight it. AI is a megatrend, one of the most foundational movements that we have seen in human history.
moments.summary: Leaders must distinguish megatrends from hype cycles — AI is a megatrend you should never fight.

moments.speaker: Jeetu Patel
moments.text: Every management book says praise in public, criticize in private. I fundamentally disagree. Establish enough trust so you are comfortable critiquing and debating in public. In private, build that trust. In public, you don't want posturing — you want problem-solving.
moments.summary: Reverse the conventional wisdom: critique and debate in public for problem-solving, build trust and reassurance in private.

moments.speaker: Jeetu Patel
moments.text: Don't delegate the storytelling. The story is not a marketing exercise after you built the product. The story is why you build the product. If you have seven or eight layers between you and the front line, you can't play the telephone game. Always own telling the story yourself.
moments.summary: CEOs should never delegate company storytelling — the story defines why you build, and telephone-game message loss across org layers guarantees failure.

moments.speaker: Jeetu Patel
moments.text: The six things you need to build a great company, in descending order: timing, market, team, product, brand, distribution. You don't have all six, you don't win. A great team in a bad market gets dragged down — the market always wins.
moments.summary: Great companies need six elements in priority order: timing, market, team, product, brand, and distribution — the market always trumps the team.

moments.speaker: Jeetu Patel
moments.text: Permission to play means asking: is this logical for us? Do we have the route to market for mass scale distribution? If you dissipate your caloric burn across too many areas, nothing gets enough girth to drive all the way through. Be extremely selective where you expend your calories.
moments.summary: Companies should only enter markets where they have permission to play and a distribution advantage.

moments.speaker: Jeetu Patel
moments.text: Persistence beats intellect, and stamina beats intellect any day of the week, twice on Sunday. The biggest reason successful leaders win is not that they're the smartest — it's that they have enormous staying power in the game.
moments.summary: Stamina and persistence matter more than intelligence for leadership success.

moments.speaker: Jeetu Patel
moments.text: The platform that you choose and the quality of problems that you pick to solve determine a lot of the path of success for you. Harder problems attract better people, and business is a team sport.
moments.summary: Choose hard problems to attract the best talent — easy problems won't draw the team you need to win.

podcasts.title: Boris Cherny
podcasts.description: This is the world now." What's the next big shift to how software is written that either your team is already operating in or you think will head towards, covering engineering tradeoffs, product design, and AI product work.

moments.speaker: Boris Cherny
moments.text: 100% of my code is written by Claude Code. I have not edited a single line by hand since November. Every day, I ship 10, 20, 30 pull requests.
moments.summary: Boris has fully transitioned to AI-written code, shipping 10-30 PRs daily without manual editing.

moments.speaker: Boris Cherny
moments.text: Productivity per engineer has increased 200%. I have never enjoyed coding as much as I do today, because I don't have to deal with all the minutia.
moments.summary: 200% productivity increase from AI coding tools, with higher job satisfaction due to reduced tedious work.

moments.speaker: Boris Cherny
moments.text: In a year or two, it's not going to matter. Coding is virtually solved. I imagine a world where everyone is able to program, anyone can just build software any time.
moments.summary: Predicts coding will be virtually solved in 1-2 years, enabling universal software creation.

moments.speaker: Boris Cherny
moments.text: Claude is starting to come up with ideas. It's looking for feedback, it's looking at bug reports, it's looking at telemetry for bug fixes, and things to ship. A little more like a coworker.
moments.summary: AI coding agents are evolving from tools to autonomous coworkers that identify and ship improvements independently.

podcasts.title: Sequoia CEO coach: Why it’s never been easier to start a company, and never been harder to scale one | Brian Halligan (co-founder, HubSpot)
podcasts.description: Brian Halligan co-founded HubSpot, ran it as CEO for about 15 years, and now coaches Sequoia’s fastest-growing founders as their in-house CEO coach.

moments.speaker: Brian Halligan
moments.text: I look for four things in CEOs — my LOCK algorithm. L is for lovable — can I envision following this person? O is obsession with the problem. C is chip on the shoulder. K is deeply knowledgeable about the domain. And I'd add S for student — the best ones are deep, deep students of the game.
moments.summary: The LOCKS framework for evaluating CEOs: Lovable, Obsessed, Chip-on-shoulder, Knowledgeable, and Student of the game.

moments.speaker: Brian Halligan
moments.text: Starting a company has never been easier. The flip side is the number of companies formed is going to mushroom. There's so much noise and competition, it's just going to be hard to stand out and really accelerate and scale. It's never been easier to start, never been harder to scale.
moments.summary: AI makes starting companies trivially easy, but the resulting explosion of competition makes scaling harder than ever.

moments.speaker: Brian Halligan
moments.text: If you want to kill a plant, have two people water it. Every CEO at the adults' table has gone through this and they are religious about the DRI — directly responsible individual.
moments.summary: Shared ownership kills execution at scale — every important initiative needs a single DRI.

moments.speaker: Brian Halligan
moments.text: EV is enterprise value, TV is your team's value, MEV is your value. Immature managers solve for TV over EV. We always put on the wall: solve for CV, then EV, then TV, then MEV. Customers first, then company, then team, then yourself.
moments.summary: The priority hierarchy for decisions should be Customer Value, Enterprise Value, Team Value, then My Value.

moments.speaker: Brian Halligan
moments.text: I always valued optionality, but there's a massive tax in optionality when you can move this fast. Planning cycles used to be a year, now they're three months.
moments.summary: In the AI era, optionality is expensive — planning cycles have shrunk from annual to quarterly.

moments.speaker: Brian Halligan
moments.text: The go-to-market is going to get turned on its head. People start evaluating products in Gemini or ChatGPT. Your website is a lot less important. Sites will change to have a high quality avatar that knows everything about your products.
moments.summary: Go-to-market will be transformed as buyers research in AI chatbots instead of websites.

podcasts.title: Sherwin Wu V2
podcasts.description: If you talk to a lot of engineers, the thing that annoys them the most is after you've written your beautiful code, how do you get it into production, covering engineering tradeoffs, AI product work, and team leadership.

moments.speaker: Sherwin Wu
moments.text: IC engineers are becoming tech leads. They're managing fleets and fleets of agents. I know many engineers on my team have 10 to 20 threads being pulled on at the same time.
moments.summary: Software engineers are transforming from code writers into tech lead managers who orchestrate 10-20 parallel AI agent threads.

moments.speaker: Sherwin Wu
moments.text: The one person billion dollar startup implies that to enable it, there might be a hundred other small startups building bespoke software. We might actually enter into a golden age of B2B SaaS.
moments.summary: The one-person billion-dollar startup will spawn a golden age of B2B SaaS with tens of thousands of $10M micro-companies.

moments.speaker: Sherwin Wu
moments.text: The Mythical Man-Month predicted software engineering would go where engineers are like surgeons. As a manager, look around corners and unblock people, especially from organizational bottlenecks.
moments.summary: Managers should treat engineers like surgeons — proactively removing organizational blockers.

moments.speaker: Sherwin Wu
moments.text: Companies where AI really works have top-down buy-in combined with bottoms-up adoption. A lot of AI deployments fail because it's an exec mandate, extremely top-down, divorced from what actual work looks like.
moments.summary: Successful AI adoption requires both executive buy-in and bottoms-up evangelism from an internal tiger team.

moments.speaker: Sherwin Wu
moments.text: The models will eat your scaffolding for breakfast. Build for where the models are going, not where they are today.
moments.summary: AI models keep consuming the scaffolding built around them, so builders should target where model capabilities are heading.

moments.speaker: Sherwin Wu
moments.text: We live in a bubble in Silicon Valley. A lot of work that runs our entire economy is not open-ended knowledge work — it's repeatable business processes with standard operating procedures.
moments.summary: Business process automation is a massively underrated AI opportunity.

moments.speaker: Sherwin Wu
moments.text: Every startup that I've seen fizzle out is not because OpenAI or a big lab has come and squashed them. It's because they built something that didn't resonate with customers.
moments.summary: Startups fail from lack of customer resonance, not from being crushed by OpenAI.

podcasts.title: The rise of the professional vibe coder (a new AI-era job) | Lazar Jovanovic (Professional Vibe Coder)
podcasts.description: Lazar Jovanovic is a full-time professional vibe coder at Lovable. His job is to build both internal tools and customer-facing products purely using AI, while not having a coding background.

moments.speaker: Lazar Jovanovic
moments.text: I don't have a coding background. My job is to build both internal tools and customer-facing products purely using AI. The key is knowing what to build, not how to build it.
moments.summary: Professional vibe coder with no coding background builds production tools for Lovable using only AI.

moments.speaker: Lazar Jovanovic
moments.text: The biggest mistake people make is starting too big. Start with something you actually need. My first project was a simple dashboard for tracking my own metrics.
moments.summary: Start vibe coding with personal needs, not ambitious projects — the learning compounds from real usage.

moments.speaker: Lazar Jovanovic
moments.text: Internal tools are the sweet spot for vibe coding. The bar for quality is lower, the feedback loop is faster, and the value is immediate.
moments.summary: Internal tools are the ideal use case for vibe coding due to lower quality bar and faster feedback loops.

podcasts.title: A child psychologist’s guide to working with difficult adults | Dr. Becky Kennedy
podcasts.description: Working with difficult adults, covering product design, team leadership, and practical product lessons.

moments.speaker: Dr. Becky Kennedy
moments.text: Repair is the number one relationship strategy we have. Secure attachment isn't defined by getting it right all the time — it's defined by an adult who's willing to repair.
moments.summary: Repair — taking responsibility after you mess up — is the most powerful relationship strategy.

moments.speaker: Dr. Becky Kennedy
moments.text: The idea of being good inside inherently requires us to separate behavior and identity. When someone's late to work a lot, start with: 'This is a good person who is late.'
moments.summary: Separate behavior from identity to have productive conversations.

moments.speaker: Dr. Becky Kennedy
moments.text: Picture a turbulent flight. The sturdy pilot says: 'I hear you screaming. It makes sense. I know what I'm doing. This turbulence doesn't scare me. I'll see you when we land.' That's sturdy leadership.
moments.summary: Sturdy leadership means acknowledging others' emotions as real without being overwhelmed by them.

moments.speaker: Dr. Becky Kennedy
moments.text: Boundaries are what you tell someone else you will do, and it requires the other person to do nothing. Too often people confuse requests with boundaries.
moments.summary: True boundaries define what you will do and require nothing from the other person.

moments.speaker: Dr. Becky Kennedy
moments.text: Optimizing for happiness in childhood is the quickest way to build anxiety and fragility in adulthood. When we're thinking about a resilient work culture, we want people who can say, 'This is hard and I can do hard things.'
moments.summary: Optimizing for happiness over resilience creates fragility.

moments.speaker: Dr. Becky Kennedy
moments.text: Dealing with anxiety is a combination of 'I believe you and I believe in you.' Picture someone in a hole — they need you to have one foot in the hole with them and one foot outside.
moments.summary: Effective leadership combines validation with confidence in someone's capability.

moments.speaker: Dr. Becky Kennedy
moments.text: The most generous interpretation — MGI. The story you tell yourself about your organization at night becomes the leader you are the next morning.
moments.summary: The Most Generous Interpretation (MGI) transforms how leaders respond to difficult behavior.

podcasts.title: Marc Andreessen: The real AI boom hasn’t even started yet
podcasts.description: Marc Andreessen is a founder, investor, and co-founder of Netscape, as well as co-founder of the venture capital firm Andreessen Horowitz (a16z). In this conversation, we dig into why we’re living through a unique and one of the most incredible times in history, and what comes next.

moments.speaker: Marc Andreessen
moments.text: Every PM in the world should be learning to build with AI tools. Not because they'll replace engineers, but because the ability to prototype changes the entire product development cycle.
moments.summary: All PMs should learn AI building tools — not to replace engineers, but to fundamentally change the product development cycle.

moments.speaker: Marc Andreessen
moments.text: The companies that are going to win are the ones where AI isn't a feature — it's the operating system. It changes how you build, how you sell, how you support.
moments.summary: Winning companies will use AI as their operating system — transforming building, selling, and support — not just adding it as a feature.

podcasts.title: 5 questions to ask when your product stops growing | Jason Cohen (2x unicorn founder)
podcasts.description: Jason’s five-step framework: logo retention, pricing, NRR, marketing channels, target market.

moments.speaker: Jason Cohen
moments.text: When growth stalls, 90% of the time it's a retention problem, not an acquisition problem. But teams always want to fix acquisition first because it's more fun.
moments.summary: Stalled growth is almost always a retention issue, but teams default to fixing acquisition because it's more engaging.

moments.speaker: Jason Cohen
moments.text: Logo retention is the most honest metric in B2B. Net revenue retention can mask churn with expansion. Logo retention tells you if customers are actually staying.
moments.summary: Logo retention is more honest than NRR for B2B — it reveals real customer retention without expansion masking churn.

podcasts.title: The non-technical PM’s guide to building with Cursor | Zevi Arnovitz (Meta)
podcasts.description: The complete AI workflow that lets non-technical people build real products in Cursor.

moments.speaker: Zevi Arnovitz
moments.text: I'm a PM at Meta with zero coding background. I built a full internal tool using Cursor in three days. It's now used by my entire team daily.
moments.summary: Non-technical Meta PM built a team-wide internal tool in 3 days using Cursor with no coding background.

moments.speaker: Zevi Arnovitz
moments.text: Start with your own pain point. Don't try to build a startup. Build something you need. The motivation to finish is completely different when you're the user.
moments.summary: Start AI building with personal pain points — self-use motivation is the key to finishing projects.

podcasts.title: How to show up in any room with a low heart rate: Silicon Valley’s missing etiquette playbook | Sam Lessin
podcasts.description: Silicon Valley’s missing etiquette playbook, covering startup building, product design, and operator lessons.

moments.speaker: Sam Lessin
moments.text: There has to be a deeper truth to it. There's a serious real narrative to why etiquette matters in 2025 for founders.
moments.summary: Etiquette is a critical business skill for founders because building trust matters more than ever.

moments.speaker: Sam Lessin
moments.text: Etiquette is a skill for how to show up in a room with a low heart rate. You kind of want to show up with the self-confidence and the calm of abundance.
moments.summary: Etiquette is fundamentally about showing up with an abundance mindset and low heart rate.

moments.speaker: Sam Lessin
moments.text: Coming in and saying 'Hey let me give you my four-minute startup spiel' is so self-centered. Think of it like ping pong — hit the ball back.
moments.summary: The best networking conversations are balanced exchanges like ping pong, not one-sided monologues.

moments.speaker: Sam Lessin
moments.text: Vibe coding is exactly good for this — Cursor and DigitalOcean and Cloudflare will get you a long way. But vibe coding also doesn't really scale.
moments.summary: Vibe coding tools are great for building personal projects and MVPs but don't scale.

moments.speaker: Sam Lessin
moments.text: Seed VCs who invest in companies branded as AI companies are going to lose an impossibly large amount of money. There's a difference between a great business using AI versus an 'AI company.'
moments.summary: AI-branded startups are terrible seed investments because they are too capital intensive and commoditizing.

podcasts.title: The power user’s guide to Codex: parallelizing workflows, planning techniques, advanced context engineering tips, automating code reviews, and more | Alexander Embiricos
podcasts.description: How to set up and use Codex in VS Code and terminal environments for both simple and complex coding tasks.

moments.speaker: Alexander Embiricos
moments.text: Context engineering is the new prompt engineering. It's not about the single prompt — it's about what information you give the AI over the entire session.
moments.summary: Context engineering (session-level information management) is replacing prompt engineering as the key AI skill.

moments.speaker: Alexander Embiricos
moments.text: The trick is parallelization. Run five agents at once on different parts of the problem. Your job becomes orchestration, not execution.
moments.summary: Parallelizing AI agents transforms the developer role from executor to orchestrator.

podcasts.title: Aishwarya Naresh Reganti + Kiriti Badam
podcasts.description: But if you make a bunch of practitioners sit together and ask them, "Is it important to build an actionable feedback loop for AI products?" I think all of them will agree, covering AI product work, product design, and team leadership.

moments.speaker: Aishwarya Naresh Reganti
moments.text: If you make a bunch of practitioners sit together and ask them, 'Is it important to build an actionable feedback loop for AI products?' All of them will agree. But almost nobody does it well.
moments.summary: Everyone agrees AI products need feedback loops, but almost no one implements them well in practice.

podcasts.title: The high-growth handbook: Molly Graham’s frameworks for leading through chaos, change, and scale
podcasts.description: Molly Graham has worked for some of tech’s most effective leaders, including Mark Zuckerberg, Sheryl Sandberg, Chamath Palihapitiya, and Bret Taylor. Today she leads Glue Club, a community for leaders navigating rapid scale, growth, and change.

moments.speaker: Molly Graham
moments.text: In high-growth, you have to hire for the role people will grow into, not the role they're perfect for today. The job changes every 6-12 months.
moments.summary: High-growth hiring: recruit for growth potential, not current-role fit — the job changes every 6-12 months.

podcasts.title: We replaced our sales team with 20 AI agents—here’s what happened | Jason Lemkin (SaaStr)
podcasts.description: We replaced our sales team with 20 AI agents—here’s what happened, covering B2B products, startup building, and team leadership.

moments.speaker: Jason Lemkin
moments.text: However you get those first 10, 15, 20 customers, be honest. If they need a sales type motion and you say 'I don't like sales, so I'm going to do a PLG motion,' you'll fail.
moments.summary: Founders must be honest about whether their customers need a sales-led motion rather than forcing PLG.

moments.speaker: Jason Lemkin
moments.text: Almost all founders are A+ middlers. Any founder should be there by day one. And prospects love it — they get to talk to you. It's magical for early adopters.
moments.summary: Founders are naturally great at the middle part of sales conversations because of their deep product knowledge.

moments.speaker: Jason Lemkin
moments.text: Those first couple reps have to be people you would buy your own product from. We're looking for pirates and romantics in the early days.
moments.summary: Your first sales hires must be people you would personally buy your own product from.

moments.speaker: Jason Lemkin
moments.text: You need two sales reps hitting quota before you're ready to hire a manager for them. If you hire a VP of sales before then, it's approaching 100% chance of failure.
moments.summary: Hiring a VP of Sales before you have two reps hitting quota is almost guaranteed to fail.

moments.speaker: Jason Lemkin
moments.text: What do you want to do your first 30 days? In B2B, if I don't hear 'I'm going to go meet customers,' I'm out.
moments.summary: The ultimate screening question for any VP hire is whether they want to meet customers in their first two weeks.

moments.speaker: Jason Lemkin
moments.text: Trust me, hire someone whose last product was harder to sell. If you hire someone from something easier, they will have none of the skills.
moments.summary: Always hire salespeople whose previous product was harder to sell than yours.

moments.speaker: Jason Lemkin
moments.text: In sales, there's rules of eight. Eight SDRs need one manager, eight AEs need a director above them. You can build your whole org with eights.
moments.summary: Scale your sales org using the rule of eight and concentrate leads on fewer, better closers.

podcasts.title: 10 contrarian leadership truths every leader needs to hear | Matt MacInnis (Rippling)
podcasts.description: Matt MacInnis is the chief product officer and former longtime COO at Rippling, a unified workforce management platform valued at over $16 billion.

moments.speaker: Matt MacInnis
moments.text: It is really important to me that we feel that we've deliberately understaffed every project at the company.
moments.summary: Deliberate understaffing prevents teams from working on low-priority items and forces focus.

moments.speaker: Matt MacInnis
moments.text: You don't really learn from your mistakes, you learn from your successes. As an early career PM, you should join a winning team.
moments.summary: You learn far more from being part of a winning team than from failure.
